titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to William J.H. Tyrrell 30, September, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: When travelling through Folkestone William Upton Tyrrell bought an electric stitch which, "is simply wonderful and will remove any stain from anything".

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
30 Sept. 1917 My dearest Daddy There is really no news of interest.
We are still in the same place. It is a
great coal mining district and all the
men are miners. There are enormous
heaps of slack all round the place
which can be seen for miles round.
Small railways run up the sides of
these and wagons full of rubbish and
slack are hauled up and then lifted
down the other side. It is quite amusing
to watch them. At present we are training very hard
for a musketing competition. The brigadeer
is giving a shield for the best platoon
in his brigade with an additional 10
frames for man for the winning platoon. There is is no use my going into the
details of the competition as I could
not explain it in paper but everyone
seems very keen on it. This morning
we were on parade at 6.15 am and
went out to the range. A good start for
a sunday morning. You see even
when we are resting we do a little
work. I did not get any letters from
you yet since I got back but no
doubt they will soon begin to
come again. There have been no additions
to the battalion at all lately
I think I have made a discovery.
I saw a thing in a slot window at
Tolhestone called electic slich
for taking stains out of clothes. As there were some stains on
some of my trench clothes I bought
a few and used it. It is simply
wonderful and will remove any
stain from anything. It is a thing
well worth knowing of in these days
when is scarce. I wrote to Bobby today. I am also
going to write to Tammy Mather
to claim by 6th which is or was down
her block. The weather is perfect here. Just
like what we have had all
summer up to August. You might send out sometime my
wooly waistcoat but there is not
the slightest hurry as this is not
the kind of weather that one would
need it. No news so must shut up. With the very best love from Your most affect Son Willie
